data.title: Micron Stock Gets Powerful Setup Ahead Of Earnings
data.description:
Elevator Thesis

Micron Technology (MU) has been a banger of a stock, to say the least.

For context, the stock is up an eye-popping 178% in the past six months alone and 370% for the year, at a time when the tech industry has otherwise been rough.

Also, since my last coverage on Jan. 2, 2026, the stock's up over 25% from its $315.42 close, as per Yahoo Finance.

Now, it's just days away from another pivotal earnings report that has a lot riding on it.

A glance at Seeking Alpha's earnings page shows that Wall Street is expecting Micron's FQ2 2026 report for an EPS (normalized estimate) of $8.60 on revenue of $19.1 billion. On top of that, over the past three months. So there have been 23 upward revisions, pointing to healthy optimism.

For some color, Micron's own Q2 guidance was $18.7 billion in sales and $8.42 in non-GAAP EPS at the midpoint. Hence, Wall Street is looking for another solid showing that continues moving the needle for the memory giant.

The bull case is a pretty simple one.

AI demand remains as strong as ever and has created a memory shortage that gives Micron a pricing advantage unlike anything it has seen before.

At the same time, AI bellwethers like GPU king NVIDIA (NVDA) just delivered another earnings smasher, posting $68.1 billion in quarterly sales, along with $62.3 billion from data centers (including a $78 million guide for next quarter).

Similarly, custom AI accelerator maker Broadcom (AVGO) posted $8.4 billion in Q1 AI sales, up 106% year-over-year, saying Q2 AI sales may reach $10.7 billion.

So clearly, the cycle isn't stopping anytime soon, and Micron stands to gain immensely from its system-level need across a myriad of tech behemoths.

Nevertheless, the stock has run hot for a while, and given the broader tech sentiment, it's important to curb expectations.

I do feel, though, given the current technical setup, earnings estimates, and momentum, Micron could potentially pop to around the $430 to $445 range, which is also supported by options-based data, but that would entail a relatively big beat-and-raise quarter and more evidence of the cycle being a lot more fleshed out than previously anticipated.

Micron's HBM Pricing Power is at the Core of It

Micron's HBM opportunity is at the heart of the tech giant's bull case.

A big part of that is because the company isn't just selling into a loose memory market as it did in yesteryears.

In its latest earnings call, Micron acknowledged that it has already secured price and volume agreements covering the entire 2026 HBM supply.

Also, it expects the HBM market to skyrocket from nearly $35 billion in 2025 to around $100 billion by 2028.

That incredible demand has meaningfully lifted Micron's margins.

For some color, its fiscal Q1 non-GAAP gross margin jumped to 56.8%, and it guided fiscal Q2 gross margin to 68.%.

At the same time, its powerful Cloud Memory Business Unit posted a whopping 66% gross margin in Q1. From a historical standpoint, Micron's gross margins have hovered in the 25% to 30% range over the past five years.

Additionally, that momentum is unlikely to stop anytime soon, and should follow a string of stupendous results from its peers.

SK hynix said its HBM sales jumped more than 50% year-over-year, lifting record annual sales to 97.1 trillion won, backed by a record quarterly operating margin of 58% in Q4.

Additionally, its Q4 operating profit skyrocketed 137% to 19.2 trillion won, with HBM demand and margin health playing a critical role.

Similarly, Samsung Memory Business posted all-time highs in quarterly sales and profit in its most recent quarter, on the back of the growing HBM sales and higher prices overall.

Third-party data confirms the uptrend.

TrendForce reports show that HBM demand surged by more than 130% year-over-year in 2025, and the outlet sees a path for a 70% increase this year.

Additionally, HBM content per accelerator is expected to rise by 20% to 30%, which means Micron's order book should continue turning heads.

Google's next TPU reportedly will be using eight HBM3E stacks, and Amazon's Trainium3 will be using four.

Moreover, as per a report from February from TrendForce, the broader memory pricing environment is still firing on all cylinders.

For perspective, blended conventional DRAM along with HBM contract prices shot up 50-55% quarter-over-quarter in Q4 2025 and projected 80-85% quarter-over-quarter growth in Q1 2026.

Micron Still Has Strong Liquidity Cushion

Naturally, given the speed at which the current memory cycle is progressing and the investments it entails, liquidity remains one of the biggest talking points for Micron and its peers.

As per Micron's latest results (fiscal Q1), things look pretty smooth.

The company boasted a massive $12 billion cash war chest, led by a powerful combo of short-term and long-term investments. In fact, its management said that total liquidity was actually even higher at $15.5 billion when factoring in an undrawn $3.5 billion revolver.

Moreover, things also look mighty encouraging in the short term.

Micron's current asset base totaled $29.67 billion, compared with $12.06 billion in current liabilities, resulting in a healthy current ratio of about 2.46x.

Strip away the inventories, and as per GuruFocus, Micron's quick ratio comes to 1.78 times, roughly in line with its 10-year median at 1.97.

Nevertheless, Micron's debt load is meaningful.

Reported debt levels were at a head-turning $11.76 billion at the end of the quarter, along with $2.91 billion of finance lease obligations. Stripping out those lease obligations, Micron's notes and term debt came in at about $8.84 billion.

As per GuruFocus, Micron's debt-to-equity ratio is 0.21 times, which again tracks closely with its 10-year median of 0.27.

Encouragingly, Micron cut its debt during the quarter by prepaying nearly 2.7 billion of principal linked with its 2028 notes, 2029B notes, and 2029 term loan. Interestingly, the company's maturity profile is also remarkably encouraging, as it doesn't have any note maturities for the rest of 2026, 2027, or 2028.

Nevertheless, the megafab story is relevant, and clearly the headline figures are enormous.

However, that's a massive 20-plus-year buildout, and it's important to note that the company has over $6 billion of CHIPS Act grants linked to those projects. So this isn't like a one-time cash bill that drains its fortress-like balance sheet.

Also, in Q1, Micron generated a handsome $8.41 billion of operating cash flow and $3.9 billion of adjusted free cash flow, forecasting $20 billion of capex, net of government incentives, for 2026.

Valuation Discussion

Micron is expected to report earnings in a few days, and as I said earlier, a lot is riding on the results.

Wall Street consensus estimates suggest the stock is fully priced ahead of earnings.

Moreover, if we look at options data from Tipranks, Micron's average one-day post-earnings move is at a sizeable 12%.

However, given Micron's incredible run-up over the past few months, nothing's a given, which is why it's important to narrow out a post-earnings target.

From the consensus EPS chart above, we can see that Micron is expected to deliver outsized earnings over the next few years.

The chart above shows that Wall Street expects Micron to post a massive $35 EPS for this year, followed by a healthy ramp-up to $47.2 for FY27.

However, those numbers are expected to plateau at around $19.6 in FY29 and hold steady the following year. Consequently, we'll see its forward PE compress to as 8.9 times by FY 2028 before rising over the next couple of years. That means there's still a ton of upside that the markets aren't pricing in as yet.

Nevertheless, the numbers also suggest that Micron is sitting in the sweet spot of the current uptrend. So I'd argue for a base-case post-earnings price of around the $430-$445 range.

Based on forward fiscal 2026 numbers, that would have the stock trading at 12.2 to 12.7 times earnings. For fiscal 2027, that would imply an even lower multiple at 9.1 to 9.4 times earnings.

However, in getting there, Micron would need a big beat-and-raise quarter, backed by stronger commentary around DRAM pricing, HBM demand, and the supply tightness to go deeper into next year. Going as far as the $500 mark would be a tough ask, as it would mean it trades at over 14 to 15 times FY26 EPS.

If we look at the technicals, Micron's weekly chart also supports my price target range.

Even after the recent pullback, the stock is still in a healthy uptrend heading into the March 18 report, trading comfortably above its 20-, 50-, and 100-week EMAs, stacked in bullish order.

Moreover, its weekly RSI reading has backed off from overheated territory near the mid-70s, which is a lot more constructive.

From a price-action perspective, Micron is apparently digesting its recent run around the $420 area. We can see support forming around the key $390-$400 zone, as the stock continues to trade near the upper end of its recent range.

If the stock holds that structure through its upcoming earnings, naturally, the next big move would be toward the upper Bollinger band, currently pointing to a $430-$445 zone and potentially even higher.

Risks To Thesis and Takeaway

Funnily enough, Micron's bear case is as straightforward as its bull case.

A big part of what weighs on the memory giant's bullish thesis is the risk that the boom we're seeing is more cyclical than permanent.

Historically, these memory-space boom cycles have mostly been temporary.

Let's look at the 2017-2019 cycle as a clean example.

Micron's sales jumped impressively from $20.3 billion in FY2017 to $30.4 billion in FY2018, while net income surged from $5.1 billion to $14.1 billion, backed by a gross margin expansion from 42% to 59%.

However, within a couple of years, that cycle turned on its head.

The company's FY2019 sales tanked 23%, with DRAM results impacted by pricing declines of around 30% due to supply-demand dynamics, inventory corrections, and CPU shortages. Similarly, gross margin dropped to 46%, while net income declined to $6.3 billion.

At the same time, Micron is going about its business aggressively, committing to a $200 billion capital outlay in targeting the relentless AI demand.

This includes a whopping 100-billion-megafab in New York, along with additional fabs in Idaho. Its rivals, SK Hynix and Samsung, have also been spending billions to bring capacity additions online.

Naturally, the concern is pretty simple: once we start seeing new capacity coming online in 2027 and beyond, the market will likely look a lot different.

That's doubly important because Micron has already priced in a ton of good news and trades at around 33 times non-GAAP earnings and 19 times trailing-twelve-month cash flows (both towering above sector and historical 5-year averages). That's pitted against a market cap that's hovering over $438.2 billion at the time of writing, as per Yahoo Finance.

So if pricing cools on the back of stronger supply, it's going to have a trickle-down effect on margins, cash flow numbers, and investor enthusiasm.

However, looking at the whole picture, Micron stock is clearly the tech anomaly worth betting on, even at current levels. Recent results from the biggest in the AI realm suggest Micron still has an incredible runway ahead and could continue to deliver record top-line numbers backed by superb margin growth.

data.title: Rates Spark: The Problem Children Get More Cranky
data.description:
By Padhraic Garvey, CFA, Regional Head of Research, Americas; Benjamin Schroeder, Senior Rates Strategist; and Michiel Tukker, Senior European Rates Strategist

The rise in US yields looks absolutely the right move, until it isn't

As feared, the two concerns that we highlighted on Wednesday only intensified through Thursday – the two problem children of higher US break-even inflation rates and higher US swap spreads. First, the 2yr breakeven is now back up to 3.2%.

Given that this is an average number over the next two years, it impliedly suggests that US inflation is heading to 3.5%, at least. Second, the 10yr swap spread is knocking on the door of 50bp. This rot is liable to continue for as long as the war angst remains elevated.

Perhaps the anomalous piece here is the tendency for real yields to rise. Short-dated real yields did fall on Monday, but are up since. Out the curve, the 10yr real yield has basically just risen. When inflation-linked bonds are bought, that should place downward pressure on real yields.

Here, it seems that the rise in nominal yield is the dominant driver, which equates to selling nominal regular Treasuries. This is not "Sell America", as the US dollar is being bought. Rather, it is the build of an inflation/deficit-rot-risk discount, with a waning rate-cut expectation to boot.

For the Treasury market bulls out there, it's unlikely that inflation expectations will tame any time soon, at least given where we are in the ongoing war. If there is going to be a material fallback in yields, it must come from a fall in real yields.

That, in turn, could be sparked by a material elevation in real growth concerns, or a jump to discount such concerns through a big risk asset sell-off. We've seen some of this, but not a big absolute flop lower. That could happen. But until then, yields ratchet higher.

Fragile risk sentiment could stop markets from turning overly hawkish on central banks

Headlines out of the Middle East remain the main driver of rates markets. As Brent has moved above US$100/bbl again with the notion of a longer disruption sinking in, inflation expectations have risen alongside, and markets are pricing in a corresponding central bank reaction.

2y EUR inflation swaps rose 14bp on the day and are closing in on 2.6%, while the market is discounting 41bp of policy tightening by the end of the year. It is not back to fully pricing two hikes, as the market did briefly at the start of the week on the back of oil prices closer to US$110/bbl.

More importantly, we saw risk-off reflexes kicking back in with more prominence. Real interest rates (calculated from the differential of nominal OIS vs. inflation swaps) were dropping along the curve, some 10bp in the 2y tenor and still some 6bp in the 10y.

More troublesome for the ECB should be that government bond spreads are coming under renewed pressure. Italian spreads were worst hit as the 10y spread over German peers widened back to 80bp.

The possible direct burden on budgets from the energy market turmoil paired with rising refinancing costs poses a clear risk to sovereign credits, though for now we suspect that moves are still more reflective of deleveraging amid more volatile markets.

Ultimately, we think the feedback loop via risk sentiment – and in the eurozone with growing concerns around widening government bond spreads – is likely to keep markets from pricing a too hawkish ECB. We can imagine a scenario where the ECB continues to signal "vigilance" throughout the turmoil, but in the end never acts on it.

Friday’s events and market views

In terms of data, we have industrial production coming from the eurozone. But given these figures will be from January, markets are unlikely to react. More interesting data will come from the US, where we have the PCE deflator for January alongside personal spending, second GDP estimates for 4Q25, durable goods orders and JOLTS jobs data.

Even though the University of Michigan sentiment survey would usually receive less attention against such a busy release schedule, that may be different this time. The survey should capture the beginning of the Middle East conflict and could give insights into consumers' initial reaction. Having said that, the end date of the survey was 9 March, and thus the impact of higher gas prices would be limited.

data.title: Nomura Mid Cap Growth Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Review

US equity markets rose in 4Q2025 amid a complex mix of AI-driven growth optimism and rising concerns around profitability, spending, and macroeconomic uncertainty. While enthusiasm around AI investment remained a defining theme, investor scrutiny increased as questions emerged regarding return on investment, balance sheet leverage, and the sustainability of elevated capital expenditures.

Macroeconomic conditions showed signs of moderation. Labor markets weakened, with the unemployment rate rising to a four-year high in November, alongside a wave of high-profile layoffs across technology and consumer-oriented companies. Consumer behavior remained uneven: retail sales exceeded expectations, supporting resilience narratives, while Visa (V) reported holiday spending growth driven primarily by ecommerce and electronics.

Sector leadership diverged meaningfully. Healthcare emerged as a top-performing sector late in the quarter, particularly within pharmaceuticals. Financials demonstrated renewed strength as the six largest US banks added approximately $600 billion in market capitalization during 2025, supported by deregulation tailwinds and a revival in investment banking activity. Housing indicators improved modestly, though affordability constraints persisted. A late-quarter government shutdown delayed key economic releases, contributing to heightened uncertainty.

Technology performance was mixed. NVIDIA (NVDA) continued to grab headlines after receiving approval to sell H200 chips to China, while ByteDance announced plans to acquire these chips as part of a $23 billion AI capital expenditure expansion. However, broader technology performance softened later in the quarter as concerns around AI-related debt and margin pressure weighed on sentiment.

The Russell Midcap® Growth Index declined 3.70% for the quarter, with communication services and consumer staples among the weakest-performing sectors, while materials and healthcare posted the strongest returns.

Within the Fund

For 4Q2025, the Nomura Mid Cap Growth Fund outperformed its benchmark, the Russell Midcap Growth Index. Sector allocation contributed to relative performance, while stock selection detracted. Information technology (IT) was the largest contributor to relative performance during the quarter, with strength concentrated in October and November. Industrials detracted throughout the period and represented the largest relative detractor overall.

From a sector perspective, the Fund outperformed in communication services and consumer staples, with sector allocation and stock selection contributing to relative performance. Conversely, the Fund's exposure to real estate and consumer discretionary detracted modestly. In real estate, positive sector allocation was fully offset by negative stock selection, resulting in a negative net effect to relative performance. In consumer discretionary, both allocation and stock selection detracted, producing a modest detraction overall. The Fund's positioning reflected a continued emphasis on bottom-up stock selection, resulting in notable overweight positions in IT, healthcare, industrials, materials, and real estate, and underweight allocations to communication services, consumer discretionary, utilities, energy, and financials.

At the security level, Coherent Corp. (COHR) was the largest contributor, benefiting from strong revenue growth, expanding margins, and sustained demand from AI-related datacenter infrastructure. CoStar Group Inc. (CSGP) detracted from relative performance, as competitive pressures and a softer commercial real estate backdrop weighed on investor sentiment despite continued revenue growth in its residential platforms.

Outlook

Looking ahead, we expect markets to remain influenced by a balance between secular growth opportunities and ongoing macroeconomic uncertainty. While AI investment continues to reshape capital spending priorities across the economy, we believe investor focus will increasingly shift toward profitability, free cash flow generation, and disciplined capital allocation.

Labor market moderation and easing inflationary pressures may provide incremental support for economic activity, though policy uncertainty and geopolitical risks remain elevated. We expect earnings dispersion to remain wide, favoring companies with durable competitive advantages, strong balance sheets, and the ability to compound earnings across varying economic conditions.

Our investment approach remains focused on bottom-up stock selection, emphasizing high-quality businesses with differentiated products, resilient margins, and long-term growth potential. Periods of volatility may continue to create attractive entry points, and we remain disciplined in balancing growth opportunities with valuation and risk considerations as we position the Fund's portfolio for the year ahead.

data.title: Six Flags: Starting Its Cedar-Fication, But Debt Knocks The Door
data.description:
Two months ago I told you that Six Flags (FUN) could be fixed even with a ton of debt after the messy merger with Cedar Fair.

Even so, I still refuse to get on that roller coaster.

It's not like I have a premonition like in 'Final Destination 3.' I just think fixing Six Flags will take a long time, and with the soft economy we're seeing out there, maybe too long for my wallet.

But of course, even if this rollercoaster doesn't collapse, I still imagine bumps along the way if they follow the path I mentioned earlier: replacing the entire board, 'Cedar-fication' of Six Flags (borrowing the term I coined for Taco Bell), and getting rid of problematic assets.

The stock seemed really cheap when I wrote my last article.

I even slapped a price target of $18 in the base scenario (upside of ~25% from where I wrote), but I didn't think it was worth the risk. Since that article was published, the stock has risen almost 7% and left the S&P in the dust during the same stretch.

And, even with a strong return for two months, Six Flags is still 25% below my initial 'Sell' rating after the monetization thesis from Land & Buildings Investment Management (an activist who was suggesting a kind of sale-leaseback or spin-off via REIT).

Neither option won me over.

It turns out that Six Flags recently sold a handful of its parks. But it wasn't quite the way I expected.

You'll understand why.

For Peanuts

I'm not talking about Snoopy and his friends but about amusement parks being sold for peanuts.

The recent deal with EPR Properties (EPR)-a themed REIT of experiential assets-wasn't a sale-leaseback (to my delight), but it valued the 'bottom performers' I showed you in my last article much lower than I expected.

Six Flags got rid of seven parks for $342 million. I had already predicted that this would happen sooner or later with two of them (La Ronde in Montreal and St. Louis in Missouri).

The deal lumped together some of the best assets in the industry-parks like Cedar Point and Kings Island-with properties that had been dragging performance for years, including the now-closed Six Flags America and a stagnant La Ronde that hasn't seen new rides in a long time.

Just out of curiosity, I also compiled how many stars each of these parks had on Google and compared them to some of the top-tier parks from Cedar Fair:

I imagined that Six Flags could sell each bottom performer at ~4 to 5x EBITDA ($65.8 to $82.3 million). What they actually did was sell each one for an average of $48.8 million. It would be at least ~25% below what I had estimated, which, between us, was already a low value.

Six Flags probably valued these parks at 3x EBITDA, which is very weak even for distressed assets.

If they actually use all that money for deleveraging, their LT debt would fall from $5.1 billion to $4.7 billion (LT debt/EBITDA of ~6.6x using the $719 million EBITDA from FY 2025).

As you can see, it wouldn't solve their problem much.

If I project the same scenario as before with 15 parks sold in the medium term, they would only pocket $732 million. The pro forma scenario for LT debt/EBITDA here is 6x after these divestments.

Cedar-fication Ahead?

Cedar-fication isn't complicated. It's simply Six Flags abandoning the discounted pass approach of Bassoul's management and becoming more like Cedar Fair.

And I'm not just talking about the gate price.

Six Flags Gold Pass in 2024 only cost $99. The top-tier pass was only slightly more expensive in $125.

Cedar Fair has more specific pricing per park.

The Gold Pass at Cedar Point, for example, was also $99 but increased to $325 with the Prestige Pass.

It works perfectly for them, since the entry level continued to compete with Six Flags and captured premium guests with a much higher per capita spending.

They also have always been less aggressive with bundles, focusing more on add-ons. This ensured them a not-so-high attendance rate but a high spending rate, which, at the end of the day, gave them higher profit margins.

Of course, I'm not suggesting they only raise ticket prices. I think that should be the consequence of parks with more in-park spending. I don't know if you remember, but I told you that the focus should be less on attendance and more on spending per customer.

Thank God, that seems to be happening.

Many of my readers and Six Flags shareholders should be happy about this.

For example, even though attendance fell about 13% compared to the same period last year, we could expect that. Passes seem to be slightly more expensive, and the economy isn't helping them. To compensate for this a little, customers spent 8% more.

I think this should pay off in the long run. Some parks should get a few tweaks here and there better food and beverage offerings, and ticket prices will become less elastic.

Take a close look at the attendance/spend per capita chart across the quarters, and you'll notice the difference right away:

I think this should continue to happen going forward, and if all goes well, in about 1 or 2 years attendance will remain more resilient with fatter margins.

Still Some Monsters in the Closet

Cedar-fication solves a handful of problems; in the short term, Six Flags is still in trouble on several fronts. One of them is that Six Flags is operationally leveraged. You can see this by looking at EBITDA, which fell by approximately 21%.

A much worse bump than the revenue drop.

Here's a chart of unadjusted EBITDA and EBITDA margin to help you better understand what I'm talking about:

Even the CEO said that in some parks (Kings Island was cited), a lot of equipment is rented, and when revenue falls, that fixed cost becomes less diluted.

When I visited Kings Island, our maintenance team shared their idea how we could save thousands by purchasing certain equipment we're currently renting. I can assure you this is something they are digging into further.

If this happens in one specific park, I imagine it happens in others as well.

This leads to another problem. With lower margins, they are generating less OCF and struggling a bit more to pay off debts.

I think this is a great time to take a look at their deleveraging capabilities post-sales.

Well, looking at the LT debt, they're sitting at something close to $5.1 billion. Since they pocketed these $342 million, if we consider they'll use all of that for deleveraging, the pro forma debt would be $4.7 billion.

But here's the catch.

Six Flags will likely see fatter margins, but EBITDA should fall, since these parks were somewhat accretive.

So, even though Wall St. is expecting something close to $3.2 billion in revenue for FY 2026, I'm sticking with ~$3 billion. The EBITDA margin, I imagine, could stay flat at 24% or even increase to 25%. Which would give something between $720 and $750 million (slightly below the $780 to $805 million I was aiming for).

If they sell 8 more parks at the same price (to meet my previous projection of ~15 parks sold), they would pocket ~$390 million and lower their LT debt to $4.3 billion.

I can also project the deleveraging coming from their FCF.

If I look at an OCF-to-EBITDA conversion of 52.6% (average of the last three years) and a guided Capex of $412.5 million at the midpoint, the FCF would be negative $25.8 million.

There is no short-term solution for this. But the right thing to do here is to cut fat, cut costs, and try to boost revenues in 1 to 2 years to dilute these fixed costs.

For example, if Six Flags actually achieves the ~$3.5 billion in consensus revenue in FY 2028 with something close to 25% EBITDA margin, the OCF would be $460 million. Here, they could reduce debt by almost $50 million annually if CAPEX remained unchanged.

Tweaks in My Price Target

If the parks were sold for peanuts, the debt they'll be sitting on will be heavier. That's quite simple. What's not easy is finding a price target for Six Flags, but I'll try.

And, I'll warn you now, it will probably be a little lower than my previous medium-term target of $18 per share.

So, if I take my FY 2026 EBITDA projection ($720 and $750 million), I still need to decide what the net debt would be here. One scenario is to consider it at ~$4.6 billion (disregarding negative FCF and looking at what they have in cash now).

I think projecting more divestments would be an overshoot.

Taking an EV/EBITDA multiple of peers like United Parks & Resorts (PRKS) and Vail Resorts (MTN), I find something close to ~8x at the midpoint.

This would give me a price target between $12.50 and $14.90 per share here.

I think you already understand that the short-term upside is almost solely in how much they could reduce debt while trying to contain the bleeding EBITDA.

I know I'm contradicting myself, but I'll give you some color on this.

If they actually sold these parks at approximately 3x EBITDA, each bottom-performing park would generate EBITDA of just over $15 million. In a scenario where they sold 8 more parks, EBITDA could fall by more than $130 million to something close to $600 million.

They would then pocket $390 million for deleveraging, and the net LT debt would be around $4.1 billion.

Perhaps that's not so accretive. Even though they need to reduce debt, improving the EBITDA of the parks or simply selling them at higher multiples would be the best decision now.

As the stock is at $15.60 at the time of writing this article, Six Flags is slightly above my short-term projection.

But I don't think we should get stuck looking only at that.

For example, if the new CEO manages to implement Cedar-fication, perhaps they could reach Cedar Fair-like EBITDA margins of ~30%, and considering the Wall Street consensus of $3.5 billion in revenue in two years, EBITDA could reach $1.05 billion.

If I modeled this even without considering any further deleveraging and bringing it to present value, the stock could double to $30.

To be quite honest with you, they depend on 'only' three things for upside: generating Cedar Fair-like margins, repaying debt as quickly as possible (preferably not selling parks for peanuts, as it hurts valuation), and dealing with operational leverage.

As I imagine the economy will remain soft in the first half of FY 2026, I'll keep the 'Hold' position here.

The downside in the short term is ~20% in the worst-case scenario, but it's not a 'Sell' because the stock could double in two years.

All it needs is a catalyst.

The CEO seems to be working on this Cedar-fication, but with attendance falling, margins aren't responding yet. I would wait a little longer before building a position here despite the asymmetry if the turnaround happens.

data.title: Housing Starts Jump 7.2% In January, Higher Than Expected
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

Housing starts jumped 7.2% in January to a seasonally adjusted annual rate of 1.487 million, exceeding the forecast of 1.340 million. This is the highest level in eleven months and marks the third consecutive monthly increase. Housing starts are up 9.5% compared to one year ago.

Background on Housing Starts

Housing starts track how many residential buildings began construction in the preceding month. The data is divided into three types of structures: single-family homes, residences with 2-4 units (condos or townhouses), and structures with 5+ units (apartment complexes). A critical aspect of the home-building industry is the powerful influence it has on the rest of the economy.

Here is the historical series for total privately owned housing starts, which dates from 1959. Because of the extreme volatility of the monthly data points, a six-month moving average has been included.

Housing Starts: Structure Breakdown

In January, single-family housing starts were at a seasonally adjusted annual rate of 0.935 million. This represents a 2.8% decline from December and a 6.5% drop from one year ago.

Multi-family building housing starts were at a seasonally adjusted annual rate of 0.524 million, their highest level since May 2023. This represents a 29.1% increase from December and a 56.9% rise from one year ago.

Note: Buildings with 2-4 units only have N.S.A. data available because tests for identifiable and stable seasonality do not meet reliability standards.

Housing Starts: The Population-Adjusted Reality

Here is the data with a simple population adjustment. The Census Bureau's mid-month population estimates show substantial growth in the US population since 1959. Here is a chart of housing starts as a percent of the population. We've added a linear regression through the monthly data to highlight the trend.

Housing Starts: A Footnote on Volatility

The extreme volatility of this monthly indicator is the rationale for paying more attention to its six-month moving average than to its noisy monthly change. Over the complete data series, the absolute month-over-month average percent change was 6.3%. The month-over-month range minimum was -26.4% and the maximum was 29.3%.

For visual confirmation of the volatility, here is a snapshot of the monthly percent change since 1960.

A Long-Term Look: Residential Building Permits and Housing Starts

In addition to housing starts, the U.S. Census Bureau and the Department of Housing and Urban Development published their findings for new residential building permits. Building permits typically preceded housing starts, offering a glimpse into future construction activity.

Despite the fact that both are monthly seasonally adjusted annualized rate (SAAR) series, they are exceptionally volatile and subject to extensive revisions. Thus it is unwise to assign much significance to a single month.

Over the long haul, however, the two offer a compelling study of trends in residential real estate, especially when we adjust the permits and starts for population growth. Here is an overlay of the two series since the 1959 inception of the starts data and the 1960 inception of the permits data.

The monthly data points are preserved as faint dots. The trends are illustrated with six-month moving averages of data divided by the Census Bureau's mid-month population estimates.

Here is a closer look at the overlay since 1990.

ETFs associated with home builders include Invesco Dynamic Building & Construction ETF (PKB), iShares U.S. Home Construction ETF (ITB) and SPDR S&P Homebuilders ETF (XHB).

ETFs associated with residential real estate include iShares Residential and Multisector Real Estate ETF (REZ).

data.title: Royce Smaller-Companies Growth Fund FY 2025: What Worked... And What Didn't
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from the Royce Smaller-Companies Growth Fund FY 2025 Commentary.

Nine of the portfolio’s 10 equity sectors made a positive impact on calendar year performance, with Health Care, Industrials, and Materials making the largest positive contributions while the only negative impact came from Consumer Staples. At the industry level, health care providers & services (Health Care), aerospace & defense (Industrials), and pharmaceuticals (Health Care) contributed most, while software (Information Technology), commercial services & supplies (Industrials), and food products (Consumer Staples) were the largest detractors.

Our top-contributing position was Palvella Therapeutics (PTX), a clinical-stage biopharmaceutical company focused on developing and commercializing novel therapies to treat patients suffering from serious, rare genetic skin diseases for which there are no FDA-approved therapies. Palvella is developing a broad pipeline of product candidates based on its patented QTORIN platform—a proprietary technology that focuses on delivering highly targeted technologies directly to the disease source. Palvella’s shares appreciated significantly in 2025 as investors grew more confident in the company’s pipeline following a grant from the FDA, patent wins, expanded indications and programs, and a positive Phase II readout, with an additional Phase III readout expected in 1Q26. QTORIN rapamycin has the potential to be the only approved therapy in multiple rare disease settings, supporting a potential $2 billion+ commercial opportunity. Palvella has recently expanded its pipeline to address additional opportunities across rare skin diseases. We began building a position in February of 2025, when it was a mostly undiscovered, and uncovered, company, with just a few sell side analysts covering the stock; today, there are 15.

Hims & Hers Health (HIMS) operates a telehealth consultation platform that provides consumers access to healthcare professionals for mental health, sexual health, dermatology, and primary care. Its shares outperformed in 2025 as the company saw positive revisions from +37% to +59% revenue growth expectations for 2025 that were driven by the company’s position in the weight loss segment. The company manufacturers a generic version of the GLP-1 via compounding operations that led its stock to rally on news that it had signed a distribution partnership agreement with leading branded manufacturer, Novo Nordisk (NVO). Shortly afterward, however, Novo Nordisk announced it had terminated the partnership. We began trimming the position on those headlines, ultimately exiting the position as our analysis showed a deteriorating risk-reward.

Impinj (PI) is a leading RAIN RFID provider helping businesses analyze, optimize, and innovate by wirelessly connecting everyday items to their systems. In short, Impinj’s tags can be attached to anything, from shipping boxes to items on the shelves in retail stores, to fresh food items in the grocery store. The tags are “read” from scanners and logged in real time on their systems, allowing customers to track data points relevant to each respective business and thus properly track and manage inventory at an item level. Impinj is a long time holding in the portfolio with a very large TAM (total addressable market) expansion opportunity, a strong patent portfolio, and an increasingly diversified customer base. After trimming our position in 2024 as their retail channel customers went through a period of digestion, we began adding to the position again early in 2025 once that period of digestion was complete, and we began to see positive revisions to earnings estimates.

AeroVironment (AVAV) designs, develops, produces, and supports the operation of unmanned aircraft systems and electric transportation solutions. The stock recovered after a sell-off on softer quarterly earnings earlier in 2025, followed by exceptional fiscal fourth quarter results (reported in April) and a spotlight on unmanned aerial vehicles becoming a larger part of the Department of Defense budget, as the U.S. looks to modernize its equipment and resources. Defense technology continues to be an area of investor focus entering 2026 and beyond given the potential of significant increases in military spending due to geopolitical uncertainty.

JFrog (FROG) helps enterprises and their software developers track, secure, and protect “binaries,” or open source code developed outside the enterprise’s servers or systems of record. The company should benefit from the expected growth of AI-generated code. JFrog reported better-than-expected earnings and revenue in November, driven by success in its Cloud and Security segments and also raised its forecast for earnings and revenue for fiscal 2025 as a whole.

The biggest detractor at the holdings level was ACV Auctions (ACVA), which provides used car wholesale auction services. Its stock underperformed in 2025 due to a deceleration of organic growth (after a robust 3Q24 of +30%), increased competition, and a continued weak used car auction environment. It remains a top holding in the Fund, given the potential for an industry rebound and several new growth drivers over the next three years.

Freshpet (FRPT), the leading “fresh” ingredient and unfrozen pet food maker, endured decelerating organic growth after outperforming in 2023 and 2024 from improved capital management and production efficiencies. The Consumer Discretionary sector struggled in 2025, and despite growing faster than most businesses in the sector, Freshpet experienced valuation contraction along with the slower growth and estimate cuts. We have used the pullback as an opportunity to add to what we think is a high quality growth company.

An innovator in the restaurant industry payment solutions market, PAR Technology (PAR) has continued to win new business from new and existing large restaurant chain customers but has also seen delays in project implementation from many customers. Its backlog continues to build, and we have used the pullback to add to our position, expecting revenue to accelerate from what should be a temporary slowdown.

Semtech (SMTC) is a semiconductor company that has been a successful turnaround after making a poor acquisition several years ago. New management have cut costs, increased exposure to data center infrastructure applications, and expressed interest in selling what is left of the acquired company (Sierra Wireless). We believe context is important with regard to this position as it was a massive outperformer in 2024, when its share price nearly quadrupled, before management announced a slower ramp in their datacenter product growth rate in early 2025, causing the shares to drop precipitously. Our timing of reducing and then adding back to this position was detrimental to performance, despite the stock itself finishing up over the calendar 12 month period.

Alvotech (ALVO) is a biopharmaceutical company that develops, manufactures, and distributes biosimilar medicines, which are lower-cost versions of complex biologic drugs whose patents have expired. These biosimilars treat serious diseases such as cancer, autoimmune disorders, and other conditions. In early November, Alvotech received a “complete response letter” from the FDA regarding its Biologics License Application for AVT05, a key product, in which the agency cited deficiencies following an inspection of its Reykjavik manufacturing site. The company then lowered its 2025 revenue and earnings guidance, which prompted us to exit our position since the investment case was based on a fourth-quarter launch of new products.

The Fund’s advantage over its benchmark came solely from stock selection in 2025 (sector allocation was marginally negative). At the sector level, stock selection in Health Care gave the Fund an outsized relative advantage, followed by stock selection in Materials and Energy. Conversely, stock selection and, to a lesser extent, a lower weighting detracted in Industrials. Stock selection also detracted in Consumer Staples (where our lower exposure was a positive), as did a combination of stock selection and a higher weighting in Information Technology.

Top Contributors to Performance For 2025 1

Palvella Therapeutics
Hims & Hers Health Cl. A
Impinj
AeroVironment
JFrog

Top Detractors from Performance For 2025 2

ACV Auctions Cl. A
Freshpet
PAR Technology
Semtech Corporation
Alvotech

data.title: Royce Smaller-Companies Growth Fund FY 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Fund Performance

In a good year for small-cap growth stocks, Royce Smaller-Companies Growth Fund (RYVPX) made an excellent showing on both an absolute and relative basis. Semtech (SMTC) is a semiconductor company that has been a successful turnaround after making a poor acquisition several years ago. New management have cut costs, increased exposure to data center infrastructure applications, and expressed interest in selling what is left of the acquired company (Sierra Wireless). We believe context is important with regard to this position as it was a massive outperformer in 2024, when its share price nearly quadrupled, before management announced a slower ramp in their datacenter product growth rate in early 2025, causing the shares to drop precipitously. Our timing of reducing and then adding back to this position was detrimental to performance, despite the stock itself finishing up over the calendar 12 month period.

Alvotech (ALVO) is a biopharmaceutical company that develops, manufactures, and distributes biosimilar medicines, which are lower-cost versions of complex biologic drugs whose patents have expired. These biosimilars treat serious diseases such as cancer, autoimmune disorders, and other conditions. In early November, Alvotech received a “complete response letter” from the FDA regarding its Biologics License Application for AVT05, a key product, in which the agency cited deficiencies following an inspection of its Reykjavik manufacturing site. The company then lowered its 2025 revenue and earnings guidance, which prompted us to exit our position since the investment case was based on a fourth-quarter launch of new products.

The Fund’s advantage over its benchmark came solely from stock selection in 2025 (sector allocation was marginally negative). At the sector level, stock selection in Health Care gave the Fund an outsized relative advantage, followed by stock selection in Materials and Energy. Conversely, stock selection and, to a lesser extent, a lower weighting detracted in Industrials. Stock selection also detracted in Consumer Staples (where our lower exposure was a positive), as did a combination of stock selection and a higher weighting in Information Technology.

Current Positioning and Outlook

Despite much noise and recent volatility, we are enthusiastic about the potential of small-caps to power ahead over the next several years. The Trump administration’s policies should continue to drive the economy and markets; it is increasingly clear that fiscal spending will continue (at the risk of growing the federal budget deficits) to rebuild aging defense and other infrastructure while developing domestic supplies of uranium, lithium, and other rare-earth materials. With a new Federal Reserve Chair looking more likely in 2026, a more accommodative money supply and rate environment may also contribute to economic growth across businesses, government, and consumers. AI, automation, and innovation of all sorts appear to be durable themes, despite the over-investment and supply chain disruptions that often occur during the early years of a major innovation. At the same time, we witnessed several pockets of speculative activity in 2025 that need to be monitored, particularly where valuations raced ahead of fundamentals, and in some cases lacked any fundamentals at all.

Our positioning continues to reflect our focus on growing companies in growth industries while managing positions (or themes) that have become overvalued in the short term. There are risks in every business and industry, of course, and we attempt to balance the risk/reward equation based on hard data, whether economic, company-specific, or valuation-related. New themes will emerge, while others will mature, a key component of our daily research efforts. It was also noteworthy that some of the highest quality small-cap growth companies underperformed during the year, offering better relative valuations than we have seen in some time, another development that bolsters our confidence in the small cap universe.

data.title: Lazard Global Small Cap Equity Portfolio Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Review

Global small cap equities rose in the fourth quarter, with signs that market leadership was beginning to broaden. Notably, the months-long AI-driven rally appeared to enter a new phase, with investors becoming increasingly cautious about the sustainability of elevated AI capex. Many perceived AI-winners (mainly companies investing in or enabling AI infrastructure) sold off as the pace of spending on chips and data centers, which has been significantly reliant on debt financing, showed no signs of moderating, leading many to question the returns such investments would ultimately generate. However, AI-linked stocks staged a recovery in the closing weeks of the period.

In the United States, investors cheered as the Federal Reserve lowered interest rates at three consecutive policy meetings despite disagreements among officials about the path ahead. At its December policy meeting, the central bank hinted that it would likely pause further cuts as it collects more data to assess labor market and inflation conditions. Investors also reacted positively to a delayed release of third-quarter US GDP data showing stronger than expected growth driven by resilient consumer spending, powering stocks in the region to new highs toward the end of the period.

Meanwhile in Europe, stocks advanced after the European Central Bank held interest rates steady amid signs that the eurozone's economic outlook was improving, suggesting its easing cycle was largely complete. The Bank of England lowered interest rates in December to their lowest level in nearly three years but signaled a slower pace of future reductions. Central banks in Switzerland, Sweden, and Norway left their key interest rates unchanged during the quarter.

In Japan, equities rose but lagged the broader global index as investor enthusiasm for the elevation of a new, business-friendly prime minister was tempered by the Bank of Japan's renewed rate-hiking cycle. The central bank raised its main interest rate to a 30-year high and signaled a willingness to tighten further as it confronts elevated inflation. Elsewhere in Asia, stocks in China declined amid weak economic data and the central bank's decision to hold lending rates steady despite the country's economic woes.

Portfolio Review

During the fourth quarter, the Lazard Global Small Cap Equity Portfolio outperformed its blended benchmark, the MSCI EAFE Small Cap Index through 31 August 2025, and MSCI World Small Cap Index thereafter (net of fees), by returning 3.0% compared to the Index's 2.8%.

Portfolio Drivers

What Helped

  • Shares of US-listed optical laser producer Lumentum (LITE) (2.4% weighting in the Portfolio) rose after the company reported solid quarterly results, driven by strong margin performance and new customer wins.
  • Coherent (COHR) (2.1% weighting in the Portfolio), a US-listed global leader in lasers and semiconductors, particularly optical transceivers (small components that reliably connect switches and servers in a data center), rose along with peers amid encouraging AI capex datapoints.

What Hurt

  • Shares of Swiss electrical infrastructure product maker R&S Group (0.0% weighting in the Portfolio) fell after management issued a downward guidance revision, citing reduced installation capacity at certain customers. We sold our position during the period.
  • Shares of US-listed mining company Ramaco (METC) (1.1% weighting in the Portfolio) fell following strong outperformance year-to-date, which had been fueled by investor optimism around the company's plans to produce rare earths.

data.title: Positive Sentiment Streak At An End
data.description:
This week's sentiment gauges saw some big moves. As we noted in last night's Closer, the Charles Schwab (SCHW) Schwab Trading Activity Index, or STAX for short, experienced a near-record increase in February.

Meanwhile, other weekly sentiment gauges have deteriorated. The AAII survey is a prime example, as bullish sentiment fell to 31.9% this week. That is the sixth consecutive weekly decline, bringing bullish sentiment to the lowest level since the week of 11/13.

That drop in bulls was met with a double-digit percentage point surge in bears. Bearish sentiment leaped from 35.5% last week, a one-month low, to 46.4% today. That is the highest reading for bears since the week of 10/16 and the largest one-week increase since November.

Put together, the bull-bear spread has now been negative (meaning there are more bears than bulls) for the fourth week in a row. The 12.1 point drop this week was the biggest WoW decline since the week of 11/13, and the spread is also the lowest since that same week.

In other words, the AAII survey has taken a decisively negative tone partway through the second week of conflict with Iran. Likewise, the same can be said for other surveys, such as the NAAIM Exposure Index and the Investors Intelligence survey.

The former showed active managers reporting as the least aggressively long since the final week of last April during the tariff tantrum. Meanwhile, bulls dropped below 50% in the Investors Intelligence survey for the first time since November. Putting it all together, our Sentiment Composite is back to negative territory following over six months of positive readings.

As shown below, this week snapped a streak of 29 consecutive weeks of positive readings in the sentiment composite. With the streak over, it ends as the fifth-longest streak on record.

data.title: Hormuz Crisis Is Forcing Europe And Japan Into Hawkish Mode: Is The U.S. Next?
data.description:
As I said in my last coverage, the reopening of the Strait of Hormuz looks quite distant.

Since then, the recent developments have only reinforced my view.

Attacks on tankers and energy infrastructure across the Gulf have continued to pile up, raising the number to 16 (with 6 of them on March 11), while oil markets are once again pricing in the risk of a prolonged supply shock.

At the time of writing, Brent is back in the mid-$90s after dropping to $80 on Tuesday. I find it quite impressive that this increase occurred despite the IEA release of 400 million barrels from emergency reserves, with the United States contributing 172 million barrels from the Strategic Petroleum Reserve.

The fear of inflation is high on both sides of the Atlantic and the Pacific. In Europe, policymakers are beginning to acknowledge that a sustained rise in oil prices could once again complicate the inflation outlook and delay any confidence around easier policy. Bear in mind the ECB hasn't cut since June 2025.

In Japan, the concern may be even more severe given the country’s heavy dependence on Middle Eastern energy imports (95% of imported oil) and its direct exposure to disruptions through the Strait of Hormuz. With 70% of Japan’s imported oil passing through this strait, I think Japan is the prime example right now for how an external energy shock can feed into domestic prices, raise inflation expectations, and push central banks to maintain a hawkish stance for longer.

I would keep a very close eye on any policy news coming from these two regions. Below, I explain why.

Europe And Japan Turning Hawkish?

As I'm typing these words, Brent oil is trading in the mid $90s/bbl as the overall situation in the Middle East appears to be moving toward escalation rather than easing.

Iranian attacks are now spreading across the energy and port infrastructure across the Gulf. One notable incident involved two fuel tankers in Iraqi territorial waters that were struck by Iranian boats. As a response, Iraq halted operations at its oil terminals.

This raises the total number of ships struck in the region to at least 16. Bear in mind that 6 of those attacks happened on March 11 alone, including a recent attack on a container ship.

That’s why I was highly skeptical in my previous coverage about a near-term reopening of the Strait of Hormuz.

Interestingly enough, some sources indicate that Iran’s crude oil exports averaged between 1.1 million barrels per day and 1.5 million bpd from February 28 through March 11. For context, the country’s average exports last year were 1.69 million bpd. So, some oil is getting out, but it's still far from the 20 million barrels per day of crude oil and petroleum liquids that transit the strait on average.

You may remember some of Trump's remarks earlier this week that tanked oil prices:

I think the war is very complete, pretty much.

We're very far ahead of schedule.

Wrapping up is all in my mind, nobody else's.

We have sanctions on some countries. We're going to take those sanctions off till the Strait is up.

In fact, in a recent interview, Trump said, "Any time I want it to end, it will end."

However, later during the same day, I noticed a different tone at his Kentucky rally: "We won," but "we don’t want to leave early," and "we've got to finish the job."

Wasn't the war "very complete"?

Well, reading Trump's comments and trading on that often leads to no good based on my past experience.

In my view, similar to what happened in the Bab el-Mandeb Strait after the Houthis' attacks in 2023/24, once the hostilities come to an end, the shipping industry prefers to take no risks (mainly due to the war risk insurance premium). Why?

Because even after the Gaza ceasefire was agreed upon in October 2025, there are still incidents like the one below on February 18, 2026:

Given that some sources suggest that Iran had laid about a dozen mines in the Strait of Hormuz, I don't think any major operator will take the risk to traverse the strait as long as the conflict is ongoing, even if military vessels (eventually) decide to escort them.

Bottom line, oil prices are up, and Europe and Japan are now preparing to respond faster than in the 2021-22 shock.

On Europe's side, Bundesbank President Joachim Nagel told Reuters on March 11 that the ECB would act decisively if the current oil shock began translating into broad inflation over the medium term.

To be clear, the impact is already showing up at gas stations, with some analysts projecting €220 extra per year in fuel spending for an average European driver if oil stays near $100 per barrel.

So far, no policymaker has called for a hike at the March 19 meeting. However, the following remarks from Peter Kazimir, ​one of the most outspoken policy hawks, raised my eyebrows:

I’d say a reaction by ​the ECB is potentially closer than many people think.

Overall, I would keep a close eye on any comments that suggest a near-term "reaction" at the March 19 meeting. As seen below, the ECB stopped cutting rates in June 2025, and a rate hike would be negative for the markets.

Moving on to Japan, back on March 3, the BOJ was inclined to hold hikes for the March 18-19 meeting because policymakers wanted more time to assess the economic damage from higher oil prices and the weaker yen.

Well, given the fact that Japan is a major energy importer, a rise in crude feeds directly into domestic costs. The problem that I see is that Japan imports close to 95% of its crude oil from the Middle East. In fact, some sources suggest that 70% of Japan’s oil imports pass through the Strait of Hormuz.

Therefore, I think Japan is the best candidate to monitor from a rate policy perspective, given its high exposure to the Hormuz disruption.

For reference, the BOJ was already hawkish since March 2024 after raising interest rates due to sustained inflation in the country. I think the hike in oil prices could be a good reason for another hike as soon as April this year.

The risk here is that these hawkish comments and policies could spread globally, eventually affecting the United States. I discuss this in the next section.

What's Next

This week, the IEA members agreed on March 11 to make 400 million barrels available from emergency reserves. As shown below, this is not the first time it has happened.

However, this 400M barrel is the largest stock release in the agency’s history.

The DOE reported that the U.S. share will be 172 million barrels from the Strategic Petroleum Reserve, beginning next week, with deliveries taking about 120 days at planned discharge rates. As seen below, the U.S. SRP stocks have declined from the 2021 levels, and at an average U.S. oil consumption of 20 million barrels/day, 172M barrels account for 9 days of consumption.

Bottom line: I don't expect oil prices to return to pre-Iran war levels due to this emergency stock release unless tankers resume their journeys through the Strait of Hormuz.

Given the escalations in the conflict that I discussed in the last section, I don't think this event is anywhere near.

Therefore, even after the February CPI print matched the Street's consensus (headline CPI at +0.3% m/m and +2.4% y/y; core CPI was +0.2% m/m and +2.5% y/y), some analysts are pushing back the date for the next Fed easing call. A notable example is Goldman Sachs, which pushed its Fed easing call back to September and December from June and September.

Since the start of the war, Polymarket is showing a spike in the probability of no change in interest rates for the June 17 meeting (the first with Warsh as the Fed Chair).

Here is where I think markets get it wrong.

It is my view that, against all odds, Warsh will cut in his first FOMC meeting. In fact, I think the current consensus of cuts for 2026 is way too conservative. As seen below, the market is only pricing in a rate cut by the December 9, 2026, meeting.

One! (notice the exclamation mark).

Given that Trump nominated Warsh and considering Trump’s (unrealistic, in my view) goal of pushing interest rates down to 1%, I believe Warsh will face significant pressure to cut rates, even if the macro data suggests otherwise. In other words, I strongly believe the Fed may allow the economy to run hot in the back half of the year.

Overall, despite the risk to the Fed’s independence, I think the markets would more than welcome a rate cut. And that's why I'm bullish for the second half of the year. The first half is very volatile, and I see two negative catalysts ahead.

I'm talking about the March PPI and CPI reports. Given the 20% increase in oil prices, I think both are going to come in hot.

That said, I expect a jump in the SPY if a ceasefire is agreed upon. That's why I took profits and closed my earlier shorts (in the form of put options) in the last days.

In the midst of the volatility, I reiterate my hold rating on the broader markets. In my view, a correction in the SPY to the 610-620 level is likely if the markets get nervous heading into the June meeting. A contributing factor could be Japan raising its interest rate, followed by (less likely, in my view) the ECB.
